paper.title: AMA-Bench: Evaluating Long-Horizon Memory for Agentic Applications
paper.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are deployed as autonomous agents in increasingly complex applications, where enabling long-horizon memory is critical for achieving strong performance. However, a significant gap exists between practical applications and current evaluation standards for agent memory: existing benchmarks primarily focus on dialogue-centric, human-agent interactions. In reality, agent memory consists of a continuous stream of agent-environment interactions that are primarily composed

memory_insight.compression_quantization_methods: Lossy compression (e.g., Huffman coding) and quantization (e.g., 8-bit)
memory_insight.before_after_comparisons: AMA-Agent achieves 57.22% average accuracy on AMA-Bench, surpassing the strongest memory system baselines by 11.16%

analysis.engineering_takeaway: Implementing a causality graph and tool-augmented retrieval can significantly improve memory system performance.
analysis.deployment_feasibility: Yes, but may require significant optimization for mobile devices (4-8GB RAM) due to high memory bandwidth requirements.

review_questions: Is this genuinely a breakthrough? Or incremental improvement?
Is the DRAM impact claim supported by benchmarks?
